user: With that visibility, would you recommend a staged demo approach (early prototype, then refined presentation) or holding resources to perfect a single demo? | assistant: Staged — especially given your constraints (contractual Q2 work, lead’s parental leave in late spring, limited budget, and a high‑visibility September demo). Staging reduces risk, surfaces usability/tech problems early, and gives you fallback content if late issues arise. Below is a pragmatic staged plan you can slot into the roadmap plus concrete checkpoints, staffing suggestions, and fallback options.

Why staged is better here (brief)
- Early prototypes let stakeholders and sales give feedback while you still have capacity to change (important before the lead’s leave).  
- Iterative refinement reduces late surprises and shortens final polish time.  
- You get working artifacts you can reuse for the client integration or product launch.  
- It enables safe fallbacks (recorded demos, canned flows) if live demo risk spikes.

Suggested 3‑stage approach and timeline (map to your Q2–Q3 constraints)
- Stage 1 — Prototype & core proof (Now → end Q1 / early Q2)
  - Goal: prove feasibility of the core interaction and key technical integration points.
  - Deliverables: clickable prototype + minimal working backend demo of the flagship flow; basic telemetry.
  - Checkpoint: prototype demo to stakeholders; go/no‑go to build full feature.
  - Who: PM + designer + one senior engineer (pair with backup); consider a short contractor spike for heavy integration work.
- Stage 2 — Refined build & rehearsal (mid Q2 → July/August)
  - Goal: expand prototype into a robust, rehearsable demo with instrumentation and error handling.
  - Deliverables: production‑like demo environment, feature flags, test data, initial rehearsals.
  - Checkpoint: internal dry run with execs and account team; confirm stability targets and risk log.
  - Note: this period overlaps the lead’s likely leave—ensure backups, handoffs, or contractors are in place before leave starts.
- Stage 3 — Polish, harden & dress rehearsals (August → event in September)
  - Goal: polish UX, performance, and the presentation; execute multiple dress rehearsals and contingency rehearsals.
  - Deliverables: final demo script, recorded fallback video, monitoring runbooks, rollback & fallback plan.
  - Checkpoint: final dress rehearsal 1–2 weeks before event; code/content freeze at least 1 week prior.

Concrete checkpoints & stop/continue criteria
- Prototype Checkpoint: prototype runs through core flow without major blockers in 1–2 end‑to‑end tests. Continue to build if OK; pivot if integration infeasible or major architecture blocker found.
- Mid Build Checkpoint (after first rehearsals): demo success rate ≥ 90% on scripted runs; no blocking bugs in the core path. If <90% or >1 Sev1 incident, remediate or cut scope.
- Final Dress Rehearsal: 2 successful full runs (live or simulated) with ≤1 minor fix each. If not met, switch to recorded/canned demo.

Staffing & onboarding suggestions
- Team composition for the feature/demo lane: PM (owner), Designer, 1–2 engineers (senior + backup), QA, and optionally a contractor focused on integration work.  
- Require 1 sprint overlap between any departing lead and the backup/contractor; budget this overlap in contractors if needed.  
- Minimize knowledge transfer pain: mandate a short “demo playbook” (run steps, test account creds, dashboards, rollback), and require unit/integration tests.

Resource & risk management
- Reserve a small contingency (from your $500k cap) for a contractor to cover the lead’s absence if needed.  
- Timebox the demo scope tightly—pick a narrow vertical or scenario that shows value without touching many subsystems.  
- Use feature flags and a dedicated demo environment to isolate demo changes from production and the client integration work.

Operational practices for reliability
- Build and test the demo flow with realistic test data and network conditions.  
- Automate sanity checks (smoke tests) that run before each rehearsal.  
- Create a clear runbook for the demo host (what to click, what to say, what to do if a component fails).  
- Prepare a recorded version as final fallback and rehearse switching to it smoothly.

Deadlines & freezes (recommended)
- Scope freeze: 6–8 weeks before event (no new features only bugfix & polish).  
- Code/content freeze: 1 week before event (only critical fixes allowed).  
- Final dress rehearsals: 2 weeks and 1 week before event; final dry run 48–72 hrs before.

Fallback options (plan these early)
- Canned/recorded demo that can be played if live demo fails (pre‑recorded but presented as “live demo” only as last resort).  
- Demo limited to a subset of the feature that is most robust (plan that subset from the start).  
- Remote video call with a technical SME on standby to answer live Q&A if the live flow isn’t used.

How to reflect in roadmap
- Mark the prototype, build, and rehearsal phases as milestones with owners and the checkpoints above.  
- Flag the demo as a hard‑visibility deliverable (stakeholder signoffs required at each checkpoint).  
- Allocate headcount/contractor budget and the required overlap time before the lead’s leave.

Bottom line
Staged approach with tight scope and early prototypes is the safer, more pragmatic choice for a high‑visibility September demo—especially with your contractual and staffing constraints. user: I want the roadmap to keep room for emergent customer feedback without blowing up commitments—how much intentional slack do you typically build into a one-year plan? | assistant: Good question — you need enough intentional slack to absorb real customer feedback and unexpected work, but not so much that you cripple progress. Below is a compact, practical playbook you can apply immediately.

Recommended slack ranges (choose per risk tolerance)
- Capacity slack (engineering/product time): 15–30% of team capacity.  
  - Lower end (15%): if platform is stable, few unknowns.  
  - Middle (20%): balanced default for most teams.  
  - Upper end (25–30%): when uncertainty, hard external deadlines, or staffing volatility are likely.
- Budget contingency (cash for contractors, tooling, emergency hires): 10–15% of your plan budget.  
- Time buffers in schedule: 1 sprint (or 1–2 weeks) buffer per quarter as a hard scheduling cushion; add a 2–4 week stabilization window around major launches/demos.

How to structure the slack (practical allocation)
- Reserved maintenance/ops capacity: 10–15% — for incidents, bug fixes, urgent customer issues.  
- Emergent customer feedback runway: 5–10% — for quick product changes, prioritizing high‑impact asks.  
- Experiments / discovery buffer: 5–10% (can be the same pool as emergent feedback if you want tighter control).  
- Contractor contingency (budget): use if internal capacity won’t cover urgent, high‑value work.

Operational rules for using slack without blowing commitments
- Protect it explicitly: make reserved slack visible in sprint planning and roadmap; don’t treat it as “leftover.”  
- Triage & SLA: define severity SLAs (what interrupts planned work) and a decision owner for urgent pulls.  
- Require a short one‑pager for any use of slack: hypothesis, expected impact, owner, timebox, rollback plan.  
- Hard cutoffs: if emergent work will consume >X% (e.g., half) of reserved slack in a month, escalate and re‑prioritize roadmapped items formally.
- Prefers small bets: only allow work that can be delivered within the reserved window or split into an MVP slice.

Scheduling patterns that absorb feedback well
- Rolling 6–8 week planning horizon: plan detail for the next 6–8 weeks; keep later work high level to accommodate learning.  
- Stagger major starts: avoid starting multiple big epics that depend on the same core team in the same window.  
- Reserve a stabilization sprint after every big launch or milestone (demo, client delivery).  
- Rotating “response” role: 1–2 engineers on rotation to handle urgent customer fixes and onboarding requests.

Governance & measurement
- Track slack utilization monthly: % of reserved capacity used, % of unplanned work, and reason categories (customer ask, incident, discovery).  
- If reserved slack is >75% consumed for two consecutive months, increase slack/budget or formally de‑scope planned work.  
- Require checkpoints: any multi‑week emergent item that drains slack must follow Continue/Pivot/Stop rules and get product+eng signoff.

How to prioritize emergent customer asks against roadmap
- Use a simple scoring triage: impact (revenue/retention/contract risk) × urgency (deadline/contract) ÷ effort.  
- If score is high (e.g., contract/regulatory or large revenue impact) → pull from roadmap and treat as hard constraint.  
- If score is moderate → use reserved slack and timebox as a short experiment/MVP.  
- If low → queue for next planning window.

Practical defaults you can implement now
- Reserve 20% engineering capacity for maintenance + emergent feedback.  
- Set 12% of budget as contingency for contractors or urgent work.  
- Insert one stabilization buffer sprint after each major milestone and one sprint of overlap for any planned role handoffs.  
- Log every use of slack in the roadmap tool and review monthly so stakeholders see the tradeoffs.

Tradeoffs to acknowledge
- More slack = more resilience to surprise but slower feature throughput.  
- Less slack = faster delivery on planned work but higher risk of missed customer needs and firefighting.

If you want, I can turn this into a one‑page slack policy you can paste into planning docs (capacity %, budget %, SLA triage, and the small one‑pager template for approving emergent asks). Which default ranges do you prefer to start with?
